summary: Jeff Atwood examines whether ECC (Error-Correcting Code) RAM is truly essential for servers, challenging the conventional wisdom that it's mandatory for reliable server infrastructure. He documents his 2016 Discourse server builds using consumer-grade i7 CPUs and non-ECC RAM, citing cost and performance advantages over Xeon processors. Through multiple academic studies, he demonstrates that soft memory errors are far rarer than commonly believed, with hard errors (actual hardware failures) being the dominant failure mode. He concludes that while ECC is cheap insurance, modern non-ECC RAM is already extremely reliable, and redundancy through multiple inexpensive servers may be more practical than making each individual server bulletproof.
key_points: Google's original 1999 server rack used commodity hardware without ECC RAM, suggesting that redundancy through multiple cheap boxes can substitute for individual server reliability
Ruby and similar languages demand exceptional single-threaded CPU performance, making consumer i7 CPUs at 4.0 GHz more attractive than slower multi-core Xeons
Modern commodity PC parts have become dramatically more reliable, with DDR4 showing zero failures in Puget Systems' 2014 data and SSDs dropping to 0.2% failure rates
Academic studies show soft memory errors (random bit flips) are two orders of magnitude rarer than previously predicted, with 70% of DRAM faults being hard/permanent errors
If ECC were truly critical to preventing catastrophic memory corruption, it would already be built into every computing device rather than remaining an enterprise-only feature
money_quotes: I see a prescient understanding of how inexpensive commodity hardware would shape today's internet.
When I get stressed out, when I feel the world weighing heavy on my shoulders and I don't know where to turn… I build servers. It's therapeutic.

Why is it that ECC is so essential anyway? If ECC was so important, so critical to the reliable function of computers, why isn't it built in to every desktop, laptop, and smartphone in the world by now?
I'm inclined to think that reliability measured by 'lots of redundant boxes' is more worthwhile and easier to achieve than the platonic ideal of making every individual server bulletproof.
Even if ECC only protects you from rare 1% hardware error cases that you may never hit until you literally build hundreds or thousands of servers, it's cheap insurance.
I deeply distrust the anecdotal voodoo behind 'but one of your computer's memory bits could flip, you'd never know, and corrupted data would be written!'

I find it very, very suspicious that ECC – if it is so critical to preventing these random, memory corrupting bit flips – has not already been built into every type of RAM that we ship in the ubiquitous computing devices all around the world.
key_insight: The received wisdom that servers absolutely require ECC RAM deserves scrutiny—studies show soft memory errors are far rarer than believed, and building redundancy through multiple cheap commodity servers may be more practical than obsessing over individual server reliability.
